title: Global Rules
subtitle: Universal rules for all languages and all projects

title: Adapter Layer
subtitle: Adapter is the data exchange hub — the only layer that knows all others
rules: All UI↔Core communication flows through Adapter
Adapter transforms data between representations — Core domain ↔ UI view model
Adapter receives UI events, validates them, dispatches to Core
Adapter reads Core results, maps to UI-ready structs, stores in AdapterState_sta
One Adapter module per major view or feature area
AdapterState_sta contains only UI-facing data — not domain objects
AdapterState_sta is persisted by Gateway on shutdown — see persistent-state.md
UI reads exclusively from AdapterState_sta — never from Core directly
AdapterState_sta is initialized from Gateway on startup
View model types are tagged `_adp` — they are Adapter-layer types
View models are flat, serializable structs — no domain logic
Domain objects (`_core`) never reach the UI — always mapped first
banned: Business logic in Adapter — delegate to Core
Direct UI↔Core communication — Adapter is the mandatory intermediary
Direct UI↔Gateway communication — Adapter mediates all state access
Adapter importing platform-specific APIs — use PAL through Core

title: Application Model
subtitle: CORE + PAL + Adapter + Gateway — stateless components, per-layer persistent state
rules: CORE = pure business logic, no UI, no platform
PAL = platform abstraction layer, stateless
Adapter = data exchange hub, ViewModel, event routing — see adapter-layer.md
Gateway = IO adapter, loads config+state on startup, saves on shutdown
UI = declarative, stateless, MVVM for all except CLI
CSS tokens and design values live in UiConfig_cfg / AdapterState_sta
CLI is the only type without MVVM — it maps args directly to CORE calls
All UI types use declarative rendering from AdapterState_sta
AdapterState_sta = UI view state (selected items, scroll pos, loading flags)
CoreState_sta = domain session state (caches, computed results, active sessions)
GatewayState_sta = IO state (connection pool, retry counts, last-sync timestamps)
All `_sta` structs implement `Persistable_x` — Gateway handles disk IO
UI reacts to changes in AdapterState_sta — observable, not polled
CSS design tokens live in UiConfig_cfg — theme switching = config reload
CORE is input to output — pure functions where possible
CORE reads from CoreState_sta, writes results back to CoreState_sta
PAL is stateless — it delegates to platform, returns result
One PAL interface, multiple implementations (Linux, macOS, Windows, Web)
File I/O, networking, clipboard, notifications — all through PAL
Gateway is initialized first — all other layers receive config+state from it
Gateway uses PAL for all actual IO — no direct syscalls
One Gateway per app — it is the IO boundary
Adapter is the only layer that imports from all other layers
Adapter reads AdapterState_sta, exposes computed properties for UI
Adapter receives UI events, validates input shape, dispatches to CORE
One Adapter module per major view or feature area
UI binds to Adapter properties — no direct state struct access
UI is stateless — all state comes from Adapter
UI sends events to Adapter — never calls CORE directly
MCP server is `_ui` — it is the AI's rendering surface, not a bus or gateway
`--mcp` flag switches the UI surface from GUI to MCP — full stack otherwise identical
banned: Mutable state outside `_sta` structs
UI logic in CORE
Platform calls without PAL
Imperative UI mutation
Disk IO outside Gateway
Import UI frameworks in CORE
Import platform APIs in CORE
Side effects without PAL
Other layers reading/writing files directly
Gateway containing business logic — it only marshals IO
Business logic in Adapter — delegate to CORE
`setState`, `getElementById`, imperative widget updates
Inline business logic in templates/views
MCP server calling Core or Gateway directly — must route through Adapter

title: Config-Driven Design
subtitle: No hardcoded values — all constants come from `_cfg` structs loaded by Gateway
rules: Every configurable value lives in a `_cfg` struct
Config structs are passed as parameters — never accessed globally
Gateway loads config from disk on startup, passes it down the call chain
Config structs are immutable after startup — no runtime mutation
Default values are defined in Gateway's load function, not scattered in code
`_cfg` structs are read-only during a session
`_sta` structs are read-write during a session — see persistent-state.md
Config struct names follow `<Layer>Config_cfg` or `<Feature>Config_cfg`
Each layer that needs config gets its own `_cfg` struct
Gateway composes layer configs into a root `AppConfig_cfg`
Gateway reads config file → constructs `AppConfig_cfg` → passes to each layer
Core receives `CoreConfig_cfg` as a parameter — it never calls a config API
Adapter receives `UiConfig_cfg` — it never reads env vars or files
Config files live in `~/.config/<app>/config/`
Gateway discovers config path via PAL — never hardcodes paths
Default config is written by Gateway on first run if config file is absent
banned: Magic numbers in Core, Adapter, UI, or PAL
Hardcoded strings (URLs, paths, thresholds, labels) outside `_cfg` structs
`Config::default()` called outside Gateway — Gateway owns initialization
Global mutable config singletons
Environment variable reads outside Gateway

title: Cross-Language Consistency
subtitle: Same patterns everywhere — one pattern learned = works in all languages

title: Diagrams: Mermaid Only
subtitle: Text-based, version-controlled, AI-readable diagrams
banned: ASCII art diagrams
External diagram tools (draw.io, Lucidchart) for docs
Image-only diagrams (no source)

title: Error Flow
subtitle: Classify -> match exhaustively -> recover or report — never crash silently
rules: Transient errors retry with backoff before escalating to SystemError path
UserErrors show actionable feedback — app stays in valid state
SystemErrors disable the affected feature — keep the app running
Bugs capture full context, invoke crash reporter, then exit cleanly
Every subsystem (feature, background task, connection) runs inside its own error boundary
A failed subsystem is stopped and reported — it does not crash the app
The app is the supervisor — it keeps running and shows which subsystems are available
Subsystems expose a state: Running / Degraded / Failed — UI reflects this state
Prefix MCP error responses with `ERROR:`, `WARN:`, or `OK:` so callers can parse intent
Include what failed + what to do next — not just the exception message
`ERROR:` blocks the action (fix required); `WARN:` informs but does not block
banned: Wildcard `_ => {}` or `default: {}` error arm that does nothing
`catch(e) {}` empty catch block
`catch(e) { log(e) }` — logging without recovery is silent swallow
Showing stack traces, internal paths, or raw error types to the user
`unwrap()`/`expect()` in application code outside tests
`throw new Error("something failed")` without a typed discriminated error
Single catch-all error type — variants must be distinguishable for recovery

title: File Size Limits
subtitle: Size follows encapsulation — when a file has one job, it stays small naturally
sections: The Mother/Child Cascade — The Only Correct Split Pattern
rules: After splitting, update all imports and references before moving on
"Approaching" means within 20% of the limit — plan the split before hitting it
Slint is the strictest — declarative markup gives AI no way to skip or summarise sections
Soft limit = warning, start planning the split
Hard limit = stop, split before writing a single new line
README, spec, and documentation files are exempt — content drives length
"Count before you write" is the habit — not a suggestion
A split always produces a folder + a mother file — never just a new sibling in the same directory
The mother file re-exports everything the old single file exposed — callers change nothing
Children are named after their single responsibility, not after the feature they came from
The folder name matches the original file name — `feature.py` becomes `feature/`
banned: Adding to a file that is already at its limit
"I'll split it later" — split at the trigger, not when it becomes a crisis
Moving code to a sibling file as a "split" — that is relocation, not decomposition
Creating a flat cluster of siblings with no mother — there is always a single composition point

title: Graph-Position Paradigm
subtitle: Every edit happens at a node. The node defines what you may touch.
sections: Why This Produces 80% Fewer Overwrite Errors
The Three Properties of a Node
Adding New Rules Under This Paradigm
banned: Editing a file because it "looks related" — related is read-only
Refactoring nodes you visited for context
Expanding scope silently — boundary conflicts are user decisions, not AI decisions

title: Index System
subtitle: Auto-generated index.yaml as single source of truth for codebase navigation
rules: ALWAYS verify index at session start (step 0 in startup checklist)
NEVER edit index.yaml manually — always regenerate
Pre-commit hook updates automatically on commit
CI fails if index doesn't match regenerated version
Submodules have their own index.yaml
Index is deterministic: same input = same output

title: Project Initialization
subtitle: Run once — when proj/ does not exist or user says "initialize project"
rules: All proj/ files are created in one session — do not leave initialization partial
Ask the user before creating proj/UIUX — confirm it is a GUI project
proj/PROJECT.Current.phase must be "1" after initialization
banned: Skipping step 0 — hooks must be installed before any code is written
Creating proj/ files from assumptions — always read the source doc or ask the user
Leaving initialization partial — either complete all steps or none

title: Know Before You Change
subtitle: 90% understanding, 10% changing — the 90/10 rule

title: Language in Code
subtitle: All code and documentation is in English — localize UI strings, never source code
rules: All code, identifiers, comments, and documentation must be written in English
Only ASCII characters are permitted in source code — no unicode in identifiers, strings, or comments
UI strings start as English literals — extract to localization files only when i18n is required
Variable names, function names, file names, and commit messages are always English
All rule files, project files, and repository content must be written in English
AI-human conversation may be in any language — all repo content must be English
banned: Non-ASCII characters in source code identifiers or comments
Native-language strings hardcoded in logic layers — use English keys, localize at the boundary
Mixing languages in the same file or module

title: Module Tree
subtitle: One encapsulated module = one file. "Nested modules" means a folder of files — never nested code in one file.
sections: Why File Size Matters for AI
rules: "Nested modules" = a parent folder containing child files — not nested code inside one file
A module may be 50 lines or 200 lines — size follows naturally from encapsulation
When a module spawns sub-modules, it becomes a folder; its own code moves to an index/root file
The working limit for reliable AI edits is ~200 lines per file
A file that causes AI context loss will produce bugs — split it before that happens
Each ES module export that stands alone is its own file
Named re-exports belong in an index.js — not mixed with implementation
Every named Slint component lives in its own `.slint` file
App-level wiring (routing, modal management) is the only code allowed in the root component file
The parent file after a split is a composer — it imports and arranges, adds no new logic
Sub-modules are named after what they do, not what they contain
banned: Multiple independent modules in one file regardless of line count
A "central" file that grows as the project grows — central files are architectural debt
Interpreting "nested module" as adding nested classes or sections inside one file
Barrel files that also contain implementation code
Growing a central `.slint` file — split on the first sign of a second independent component

title: Type Naming — Layer Tag Suffix
subtitle: Every public type carries its layer tag — `BuilderAdapter_adp`, `CoreState_sta`, `AppConfig_cfg`
rules: Suffix is appended to the PascalCase type name — `TypeName_tag`
`grep _adp` finds every Adapter type across all files — the tag is the search key
`_sta` and `_cfg` override the layer tag — state and config structs use their own suffix regardless of layer
When in doubt where a type belongs — pick the layer, apply the tag, move it to the matching folder
If a type spans two layers, move it to `_x` (shared)
banned: Public types without a layer tag suffix
Tag and folder disagreeing — `_core` type in `src/adapter/` is a placement error

title: Flat Code
subtitle: Max 3 nesting levels — early returns, extract helpers
rules: Max 3 nesting levels — extract to helper function if deeper
Early returns to reduce nesting — guard clauses at top
Extract helpers for complex logic — name the intent
If/else on same level, not nested
banned: Deep nesting (4+ levels)

title: Persistent AI Memory
subtitle: RAG + FIXES = AI's persistent memory across sessions

title: Persistent State
subtitle: Per-layer state structs — Gateway handles all disk IO, layers own their state
rules: State structs are tagged `_sta` — this is distinct from the layer tag
`<Layer>State_sta` prefix identifies the owning layer (AdapterState_sta, CoreState_sta)
Gateway loads all state on startup, distributes to layers as parameters
Gateway saves all state on shutdown (or explicit flush)
Layers never call disk IO directly — they hand state back to Gateway
`Persistable_x` trait is the contract between Gateway and state structs
If a value changes while the app runs → it's state (`_sta`)
If a value only changes between runs (via config file edit) → it's config (`_cfg`)
State struct name = `<OwnerLayer>State_sta`
Prefix shows ownership — `grep <Layer>State` finds the owner immediately
`grep _sta` finds all state structs across all layers
Each `_sta` struct implements `Persistable_x`
Gateway collects all `_sta` refs at startup registration — no runtime discovery
Config files in `~/.config/<app>/config/` — read-only during session
State files in `~/.config/<app>/state/` — read on startup, written on shutdown
Gateway discovers paths via PAL — never hardcodes `~/.config`
banned: Global mutable state
Disk access (file read/write) outside Gateway
State embedded in domain objects (`_core` types hold domain data, not session state)
State stored in UI components — UI is stateless, state lives in `_sta` structs

title: Read Before Write
subtitle: Never modify files without reading them first

title: Secrets Location
subtitle: Central secrets, never copied into projects
rules: Use directly from ~/.env/ — never copy secrets into projects
Reference via symlink or env var, not duplication
banned: Copying .env files into project folders
Secrets in git repos (even gitignored copies)

title: Mandatory Startup Checklist
subtitle: Do this BEFORE answering the user — every session
rules: All project files live in `proj/` — never in the project root
Validate that proj/TODO phase matches proj/PROJECT Current.phase
If proj/ does not exist → follow global/initialize.md — do not improvise

title: Technical Debt Markers
subtitle: No debt markers in committed code — fix it or file a ticket
banned: `TODO` comments in committed code — create a ticket instead
`FIXME` comments — fix before committing
`HACK` or `WORKAROUND` comments — refactor instead
`NOCOMMIT` — must never reach a commit
`XXX` markers — use a ticket

title: Application Topology
subtitle: 6-layer hexagonal MVVM — every file has a home, every import has a direction
rules: Each type's suffix tag matches its folder (e.g. `_adp` lives in `src/adapter/`)
Folder name maps directly to suffix tag — no ambiguity
Gateway is the only layer that touches external IO (disk, network, processes)
Adapter is the only layer that imports from all other layers
A type tagged `_adp` lives in `src/adapter/` — tag and folder always agree
State structs use `_sta` tag regardless of layer — see persistent-state.md
Config structs use `_cfg` tag regardless of layer — see config-driven.md
GUI and MCP are both `_ui` — alternative rendering surfaces for the same Adapter state
`--mcp` flag switches the UI surface from GUI to MCP — Core, Gateway, PAL are identical in both modes
UI ↔ Adapter (events up, computed props down)
Adapter → Core (dispatch); Core → Adapter (results/reads)
Core → PAL (platform operations from business logic)
Adapter → Gateway (state read/write)
Gateway → PAL (disk/network IO via platform abstraction)
PAL → Platform APIs (iOS, Android, Windows, Linux — all platform targets are PAL implementations)
Shared (`_x`) may be imported by any layer
`_sta` and `_cfg` types follow their host layer's import rules
A `_core` file importing a `_adp` type is always a placement error — move the logic up
New type → pick folder → apply matching tag → done
If a type spans two layers, split it or move it to `_x`
Tests live in `tests/` mirroring `src/` — test types use `_test` tag
banned: Circular imports between layers
UI importing Core directly — all communication goes through Adapter
Core importing UI, Adapter, or Gateway
Types living outside their designated folder
Core → Adapter, Core → UI, Core → Gateway (direct)
UI → Core (must go through Adapter)
PAL → Core, PAL → Adapter, PAL → UI, PAL → Gateway
`_ui` file importing a `_core` type — UI must not know Core exists
`_ui` file importing a `_pal` type — UI must not know platform exists
`_ui` file importing a `_gtw` type — UI must not know IO exists
`_core` file importing a `_adp` type — Core must not know Adapter exists
`_core` file importing a `_ui` type — Core must not know UI exists
`_core` file importing a `_gtw` type — Core must not know IO exists
`_pal` file importing a `_core` type — PAL must not know domain exists
`_pal` file importing a `_adp` type — PAL must not know Adapter exists
`_pal` file importing a `_ui` type — PAL must not know UI exists
`_pal` file importing a `_gtw` type — PAL must not know Gateway exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_adp` type — Gateway must not know Adapter exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_ui` type — Gateway must not know UI exists
`utils/`, `helpers/`, `misc/` folders — every file belongs to a layer
Adapter logic in Core or PAL

title: Validation Over Abstraction
subtitle: Never hide complexity behind abstractions — expose and check
rules: Install rulestools in every project — scan on every commit, watch on every edit
Fix all errors before committing — warnings inform, errors block

title: Modern Language Versions
subtitle: Not outdated, not bleeding edge — the middle path
rules: Use highest version all modern browsers/runtimes support
AI knows latest LTS best — use it
banned: Legacy syntax for old browser support (unless explicit requirement)
Bleeding edge proposals not yet stable

title: Project Files Specification
subtitle: Standard project files for AI-assisted development — all live in `proj/`, no file extension
rules: Create `proj/` on project init if it does not exist
`proj/` is excluded from the public repo — it is private dev state
banned: Placing project files in project root
Adding any extension (.md, .yaml, .txt) to project files

title: AUTHORS File
subtitle: Contributors to the project — human and AI
rules: File lives at `proj/AUTHORS`
Credit both human and AI contributors
Include roles and contributions
Update when team or AI assistants change

title: CHANGELOG File
subtitle: User-facing release notes — NOT for internal task tracking
rules: File lives at `proj/CHANGELOG`
Only update CHANGELOG.md on release/deploy — not during development
Summarize what users see and experience — not implementation details
Newest release at TOP — append-only
Use semantic versioning (vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH)
CHANGELOG.md version must match package metadata version on release
CHANGELOG.md is published to the public repo — DONE.md stays private

title: DONES Folder
subtitle: Completed TODO files — append-only archive folder
rules: Folder lives at `proj/DONES/`
Each file in DONES/ is a completed TODO file dropped in as-is
Never edit files already in DONES/ — the folder is append-only
DONES/ grows forever — it is the project history
Naming: use the original TODO filename plus phase/date for disambiguation
Do not summarize or transform TODO content when archiving — dump the file as-is
If a TODO was only partially done when replaced, note `archived:` date at top

title: FIXES File
subtitle: Problem-solution pairs — AI reads before coding, writes after fixing
sections: 2026-01-20: CSS grid breaks on mobile Safari
rules: File lives at `proj/FIXES`
AI MUST read FIXES.md before coding — every session
Every resolved ISSUES.md entry → add entry to FIXES.md
Newest entries at TOP
Problem → Cause → Solution format — never skip Cause
`Cause:` is mandatory — without it the fix cannot be understood or reused
`Solution:` must reference the specific file/line changed where relevant
Short title on `##` heading — enough to find it by scanning

title: Goal Chain
subtitle: PROJECT Goal is the foundation — everything traces back to it
rules: Every milestone must trace to a sentence in PROJECT ## Goal
A phase without a milestone is just work — not progress
Goal text changes rarely. Milestones are set when phases are planned
`pass:` lives only in TODO — it is binary, you pass or you don't
When all `pass:` criteria pass → milestone done → phase done → DONE

title: INSTALL File
subtitle: Setup and publishing instructions — from private DEV to public install
rules: File lives at `proj/INSTALL`
Never publish `proj/` folder to public repo — it stays private
Verify no secrets in public repo before every push
Test install from public repo on a clean machine
DEV identity and installed identity must be distinct when applicable
CHANGELOG.md version must match package version on release
Keep local setup section minimal and copy-pasteable

title: ISSUES File
subtitle: Problem queue — FIFO order, no skipping
sections: 2026-02-24: CSS grid breaks on mobile Safari
rules: File lives at `proj/ISSUES`
AI discovers issue → add to ISSUES.md, do NOT fix without user approval
FIFO order — handle oldest open issue first, no skipping
Resolved issues MUST have a corresponding FIXES.md entry
Resolved issues move below `# --- DONES ---` line

title: PHASES File
subtitle: Milestone reference — what the project delivers and when
rules: File lives at `proj/PHASES`
Each phase must have a `milestone:` that traces to PROJECT.md Goal
`delivers:` lists concrete outputs — not vague descriptions
Only ONE phase may be `active` at a time
Active phase must match PROJECT.md current phase and TODO.md phase
User owns PHASES.md — AI reads for overview, suggests updates
When phase completes → move below DONES, update PROJECT.md, archive TODO.md to DONE.md

title: PROJECT File
subtitle: Project identity and state — what's built, where we are, what's planned
sections: PROJECT Is Single Source of Truth
rules: AI reads proj/PROJECT first, every session — before any work
AI maintains proj/PROJECT — keeps it current after every significant change
User reviews and approves updates to proj/PROJECT
AI never changes the `## Goal` section without explicit user approval
External docs (doc/, README) may exist for humans — AI ignores them in favour of proj/PROJECT
Read the source document once — extract goal, stack, structure, patterns, and decisions
Write everything extracted into proj/PROJECT in the standard format
Everything that comes up during development is added to proj/PROJECT, not to the source document
After bootstrapping, the source document is frozen — it is not updated, not referenced again
banned: Storing architectural decisions, stack choices, or project state anywhere except proj/PROJECT
`doc/project.md`, `README.md`, or any file outside `proj/` as ongoing architectural reference
Treating the bootstrap source as a living document alongside proj/PROJECT
Splitting truth between the source document and proj/PROJECT at any point after init

title: Project Process — State Machine
subtitle: TODO and ISSUES are living queues — items flow in constantly, completed items flow out
rules: One task `in_progress` at a time per AI session
Items move DOWN across DONES — never back up
Moving below DONES means evaluated and closed — no re-opening
`TODO.phase` must match `PROJECT.Current.phase`
`TODO.id` must match `PROJECT.Current.id`
Active phase in PHASES must match PROJECT phase
If any check fails → STOP and ask user before proceeding

title: RAG File
subtitle: Project knowledge base — discoveries, links, patterns
rules: File lives at `proj/RAG`
Organized by category — one `##` heading per topic area
Short, precise entries — lookup reference, not prose
AI writes discoveries here during work
AI reads RAG.md for context before starting new areas
Only write facts that are true now — remove stale entries
Link to FIXES.md for problems already solved (`see FIXES YYYY-MM-DD`)

title: RULES File
subtitle: AI's rule observations for this project — active MCP rules + project-specific conventions
sections: Bootstrap — how to derive Active Rules for a new project
rules: File lives at `proj/RULES`
AI reads proj/RULES at session start — immediately after proj/PROJECT
AI writes to proj/RULES when it discovers a project-specific convention
AI writes to proj/RULES when a FIXES entry implies a recurring rule
When a rule is superseded or wrong, AI updates it — proj/RULES is a living document
Active MCP rules (which files to load this phase) live here, not in proj/PROJECT
The resulting list must feel like it was written for THIS project — not a generic dump
Phase 1 (bootstrap): load global + language READMEs. Phase 2+ (active dev): load topic-specific files as work progresses.
If in doubt — a rule is a RULE, a fact is RAG, a bug story is FIXES
banned: Putting project-specific rules only in RAG — rules go in RULES, discoveries go in RAG
Letting proj/RULES grow stale — if a rule no longer applies, remove it

title: TODO File
subtitle: Current phase tasks — tracks work, status, and pass criteria
rules: Primary file lives at `proj/TODO`
`phase` and `id` at top MUST match PROJECT current phase
Tasks MUST belong to the current phase — no scope creep
AI updates task status as work progresses
When all tasks in a TODO file are done → move the file to `proj/DONES/` and create a new TODO
`pass:` defines the success criterion — how to verify the task is truly done
Additional TODO files may be named `proj/<NAME>-TODO` for parallel workstreams
`proj/TODO` is always the top task truth — other TODO files are subordinate
One task `in_progress` at a time per AI session
`pass:` is binary — it either passes or it doesn't
Completed tasks are removed when the whole TODO file moves to `proj/DONES/`
New tasks discovered during work go to ISSUES first, not directly to TODO
Keep TODO files to a manageable size — split into named TODO files if needed

title: UIUX File
subtitle: UI/UX source of truth for this project — platform, architecture, flows, and conventions
rules: AI reads proj/UIUX at session start alongside proj/PROJECT and proj/RULES
AI updates proj/UIUX when UI/UX decisions are made or conventions discovered
User defines the vision sections — AI never changes Goal, Platform, or Flows without approval
When proj/UIUX contradicts a UI file, fix the UI file — UIUX wins
A project targeting 3 platforms loads exactly 3 platform files — not "uiux/ (all)"
banned: Making UI layout or UX decisions not grounded in proj/UIUX
Skipping proj/UIUX because "it's a small change" — all GUI changes require it

title: Workflow, Relations, and Publishing
subtitle: How files connect, who owns what, and how work flows from start to publish
rules: Private repo is source of truth for development
Public repo is clean, publishable subset
Never push proj/ to public repo

subtitle: Making AI coding assistants context-aware from session start

title: Language Detection
subtitle: Detect project languages and load relevant rules

title: Maintenance
subtitle: Keep project files updated for maximum AI effectiveness
rules: The more you maintain these files, the smarter AI becomes on your project

subtitle: Run a script when session starts — output becomes AI context

title: System Prompt Injection
subtitle: Prepend context to system prompt for API-based tools

subtitle: Implementation examples for specific AI tools

title: AI:DevOps Workflow
subtitle: UI-first development — build from screen to server

title: CI/CD Pipeline
subtitle: Tag-triggered builds from clean checkout — artifacts go to release, never manual upload
rules: All release builds from clean CI checkout — no local state
Tag-triggered releases only (pattern: `v*`)
Matrix builds for all target platforms
Artifacts uploaded to release automatically
Pin Qt version — do not use `latest`
Cache Qt install (saves ~3 min per build)
Use platform deploy tools (`linuxdeployqt`, `windeployqt`, `macdeployqt`)
Cache dependency installs (Qt, vcpkg, pip, npm)
Never cache build artifacts across releases
Use `actions/cache@v4` with version-pinned keys
Release title = tag name (e.g., `v0.1.0`)
Artifact names include platform and version: `project-0.1.0-linux-amd64.deb`
banned: Manual artifact uploads to releases
Builds from dirty worktrees or local build/ directories
Secrets in workflow files — use repository secrets only
Skipping tests in release builds
Generic artifact names like `build.zip` or `output.tar.gz`

title: Commit Discipline
subtitle: One task, one commit. Git history is the audit trail of what works.
rules: Max 10 files per commit — if staged > 10 files, split into logical sub-commits
Call check_staged() before every git commit to verify file count
Commit trigger = task complete + tests pass — not "end of session"
WIP commits use prefix `wip/<phase>:` — valid for mid-task checkpoints that compile
banned: Committing everything at once after a long session — split by logical unit first
Skipping check_staged() before git commit

title: Package Formats
subtitle: Platform-specific packaging — DEB, RPM, MSI, DMG, tar.gz, APK, IPA
rules: One package = one app (no bundling unrelated tools)
Package name lowercase with hyphens: `my-app`
Include LICENSE in every package format
Version from git tag — single source of truth
Install binaries to `/usr/bin/`
Desktop entries to `/usr/share/applications/`
Icons to `/usr/share/icons/hicolor/{size}/apps/`
App data to `/usr/share/{package-name}/`
Same install paths as DEB (`/usr/bin/`, `/usr/share/`)
Use CPack for dual DEB+RPM generation from same build
Use WiX Toolset v5 (modern, open-source)
GUI apps get Start Menu shortcut
CLI apps add install dir to PATH
Set `UpgradeCode` GUID once — never change it
Use `MajorUpgrade` for clean version upgrades
Top-level directory named `{name}-{version}/`
Binary in `bin/` subdirectory
Always include README and LICENSE
GUI apps use `.app` bundle inside DMG
CLI tools use tar.gz instead
Code sign with `codesign --deep --strict`
Notarize with `xcrun notarytool submit`
Build with Gradle (`assembleRelease` / `bundleRelease`)
AAB for Play Store, APK for sideload/direct distribution
Sign with release keystore (never debug keystore)
Set `minSdk` and `targetSdk` explicitly in `build.gradle.kts`
Build with `xcodebuild archive` then `xcodebuild -exportArchive`
Use provisioning profile for distribution (App Store or Ad Hoc)
Manage certificates and profiles via CI secrets
banned: Hardcoded versions in package metadata (derive from tag)
Packages without uninstall capability
Files in `/usr/local/` (reserved for manual installs)
Files in `/opt/` unless self-contained bundle with justification
Installers without uninstall support
Writing to `C:\Program Files` without proper permissions
Tar bombs (files extracted to current directory without wrapper dir)
Unsigned DMGs for distribution (Gatekeeper blocks them)
Debug keystore in release builds
Hardcoded signing credentials in build files
Manual code signing on developer machine for releases

title: Private-to-Public Publishing
subtitle: Separate private dev repo from clean public release repo
rules: Private repo holds ALL code — source, tests, proj/, docs, dev scripts
Public repo holds ONLY distributable content — source, build config, LICENSE, README
Public repo can be open-source OR private/commercial
Use `bin/publish.sh` to sync private to public repo
Script uses rsync with explicit excludes — no manual copying
Always review diff in public repo before committing
Use semantic versioning — `v{MAJOR}.{MINOR}.{PATCH}`
Tags trigger CI release builds (tag pattern: `v*`)
MAJOR = breaking changes, MINOR = new features, PATCH = fixes
First release is `v0.1.0` (pre-1.0 = unstable API)
banned: Institution files (proj/) in public repo
Dev scripts, .claude/, doc/ in public repo
Test suites in public repo (unless library with user-facing tests)
Untagged releases
Manual version bumps in multiple places — single source of truth

title: UI-First Workflow
subtitle: Start with UI, work backwards to endpoint — every step visible and testable
rules: Visible result in UI (screen or flow can be demonstrated)
A test or manual reproducible checklist per step
Clear notes about next step and dependencies

title: IPC Contract
subtitle: Unix socket + JSON-RPC 2.0 — shared by all service layers

title: IPC Contract — Unix Socket + JSON-RPC 2.0
subtitle: Shared by all service layers (Python, Node.js, Rust)
rules: Bind to `127.0.0.1` by default; expose to LAN only when required
Use firewall rules to restrict inbound connections
Add authentication if exposed beyond localhost
Consider TLS or SSH tunneling for remote access
All application-level results must follow ACK pattern
Do not mix error styles inside `result`; protocol errors go to `error`
MCP uses stdio transport (not Unix sockets) — see mcp/ category
Each JSON-RPC message is a single line
The receiver must ignore blank lines
Messages must not contain unescaped newlines

title: MCP — AI Integration Category
subtitle: Rules for exposing your app to AI assistants via the Model Context Protocol

title: MCP App Server — UI Component
subtitle: `app --mcp` gives AI the same access as a human user — MCP is the AI's user interface
rules: App supports `--mcp` CLI flag to start in MCP mode — MCP UI instead of GUI
In `--mcp` mode: MCP UI + Adapter + Core + Gateway + PAL — no GUI layer initialised
MCP uses stdio transport — the app is launched as a subprocess by the AI client
MCP tool names map directly to Adapter event names
MCP server is introduced early in the project — gives AI test access before GUI exists
`--mcp` flag is parsed in main/PAL — before any GUI initialisation
MCP reads application state from `AdapterState_sta` — same source as GUI
Do not implement MCP over Unix sockets or TCP — stdio is the required transport
One MCP server instance per app process — started by the `--mcp` flag
MCP `_ui` module fires events to Adapter (`_adp`) — events up, data down
MCP `_ui` module reads computed properties from Adapter (`_adp`) — same as GUI bindings
MCP tool list and Adapter event list must stay in sync — a missing tool is an architecture gap
MCP tool descriptions reflect Adapter event semantics — not UI label text
MCP tools may read `AdapterState_sta` — same source as UI
MCP tools may expose log output, console output, and diagnostic files as tool results
MCP tools may read arbitrary files via Gateway's existing file-access surface
MCP tool results reflect the real app state after the event has been processed
banned: MCP server calling Core directly — must go via Adapter events
MCP server calling Gateway directly — must go via Adapter events
MCP tool without a corresponding Adapter event
GUI initialisation in MCP mode
Business logic in the MCP UI module
MCP `_ui` module importing Core (`_core`) directly — must route through Adapter
MCP `_ui` module importing Gateway (`_gtw`) directly — must route through Adapter
MCP `_ui` module importing PAL (`_pal`) directly
MCP tools caching state independently of `AdapterState_sta`

title: Adapter Event API — Single Input Layer
subtitle: Every input source — button, keyboard shortcut, MCP tool — fires the same named event
rules: Events are named by intention, not by input source (`on_save` not `on_ctrl_s_pressed`)
All input sources (button, keyboard shortcut, MCP tool) register on the same named event
One logic callback per named event — never duplicated across input sources
Input sources know only the event name — they do not know each other
The logic layer is unaware of input source (full decoupling)
When naming an event, ask "what does this *do*?" not "what triggered it?"
MCP tool names are derived from Adapter event names — they are not independent identifiers
banned: Button handler calling Core directly — bypasses the Adapter event API
Event names derived from input source (`on_ctrl_s`, `on_save_button_clicked`)
Same logic implemented in both a button handler and an MCP tool

title: Personas
subtitle: Pre-prompt definitions for specialized AI developer roles. Each persona = behavioral spec + workflow rules + referenced rule files.

title: Persona: AIDevOps
subtitle: System prompt template. Strip YAML frontmatter and paste as Claude system prompt. Load alongside required rules: project-file.md, phases-file.md, todo-file.md.

title: Persona: Dev
subtitle: Pure execution persona. Reads PHASES and TODO, executes tasks, moves done to DONE. No planning, no architecture, no scope changes — only delivery. System prompt template: strip YAML frontmatter and paste as Claude system prompt.

title: Persona: OPS
subtitle: Pure collaborative planning persona. No code — only project documentation and iterative paradigm development together with the human. System prompt template: strip YAML frontmatter and paste as Claude system prompt.

title: Persona & Agent Prompt Engineering
subtitle: Rules for writing persona files and agent prompts that work reliably with Claude. Source: Anthropic prompt engineering best practices (claude.ai/docs).
sections: Agentic Persona Rules (from Anthropic agentic systems guidance)
How Persona Files in This Repo Work
rules: Provide context/motivation for constraints — not just "never X" but "never X because Y"
Use numbered steps for ordered sequences — Claude follows them precisely
Tell Claude what to do, not only what not to do — positive framing first
Put long context (documents, state) before instructions — improves response quality up to 30%
Wrap examples in `<example>` tags (multiple in `<examples>`) — separates them from instructions
Role section = one sentence — focus, not a paragraph
Constraints section explains WHY each limit exists — Claude understands and generalizes
Examples section uses `<example>` tags — 3–5 examples for best results
"You are [Name], [one-line description of what this AI does]."
The role sentence establishes tone, expertise, and behavioral baseline immediately
Name the persona — a named persona is more consistent than an unnamed role
Each hard constraint states its reason — one sentence is enough
Use "never" and "always" sparingly — reserve for genuinely non-negotiable constraints
Use git for state tracking — git log is the record of what has been done
Tests before commits — write tests first, commit only when they pass
Incremental progress — advance steadily on a few things at a time, not everything at once
Save state before context window fills — write progress to files, commit, then continue fresh
Make independent tool calls in parallel — sequential only when one depends on another
Every persona file must have a `requires:` list in frontmatter — these are the rule files
The persona body uses XML tags for all major sections
RULE/VITAL/BANNED markers in the body are enforceable — they also appear in register.jsonl
banned: Writing a persona as a flat wall of text with no XML structure
Overengineering — no abstractions, helpers, or features beyond what was asked
Hard-coding to pass tests — implement the real logic, not the minimal case
Speculating about code before reading it — read first, answer second

title: Gateway IO
subtitle: Gateway is the only layer that touches disk, network, and processes — everything else is pure
rules: Gateway delegates all actual IO to PAL — no direct syscalls, no `std::fs` outside Gateway
Gateway functions take `_cfg` and `_sta` types as parameters — no global state
One Gateway module per IO concern — `RuntimeGateway_gtw`, `PublishGateway_gtw`, `McpGateway_gtw`
Path resolution always goes through PAL — never hardcode `~/.config` or platform paths
Gateway structs hold a PAL reference — IO calls go through it, not through `std` directly
Each Gateway method does one IO operation — no compound multi-step methods that mix concerns
banned: `std::fs::read`, `std::fs::write`, `reqwest::get` outside Gateway
`unwrap()` on any IO operation — surface all errors as `Result`
Gateway modules containing business logic — IO marshalling only
`_gtw` file importing a `_adp` type — Gateway must not know Adapter exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_ui` type — Gateway must not know UI exists

title: Gateway Lifecycle
subtitle: Gateway is initialized first and shut down last — it owns every layer's state on disk
rules: Gateway returns fully parsed `_cfg` and `_sta` structs to the caller — layers receive, not fetch
Gateway collects all `_sta` registrations at init time — no runtime state discovery
If a state file does not exist, Gateway returns the default `_sta` — never an error
Partial state corruption → log warning, use default — never abort the whole app
banned: Layers fetching their own config or state from disk — Gateway hands it to them
Writing state mid-session from outside Gateway — flush via Gateway only
`_gtw` file importing a `_adp` type — Gateway must not know Adapter exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_ui` type — Gateway must not know UI exists

title: Adapter Event Flow
subtitle: Receive UI event → validate input → dispatch to Core → map result → push complete state to UI
rules: Event handler steps in order: validate input shape → dispatch to Core → map result to _adp → push state
Input shape validation belongs in Adapter — Core trusts its inputs and enforces business rules
All `ui.on_*()` registrations happen before `ui.run()` — never registered lazily or conditionally
Adapter::init() receives `ui`, `core`, and `state` — wires them together, returns self
banned: Business logic in event handlers — one call to Core, no conditions around it
Pushing partial state updates — always push the whole `AdapterState_sta` after a change
Event handlers that read from `ui.get_*()` to decide what to do — state lives in Adapter, not UI

title: Adapter ViewModel
subtitle: Domain objects stop at the Adapter boundary — UI only sees flat, UI-ready structs
rules: View model types are flat, serializable structs tagged `_adp` — no methods, no domain logic
One `AdapterState_sta` per Adapter module — it contains exactly what that view needs
Map at the Adapter boundary — Core returns `_core`, Adapter converts to `_adp` immediately
Status flags (`is_loading`, `error_message`, `is_empty`) are always explicit in `AdapterState_sta`
`AdapterState_sta` is initialized from Gateway on startup and persisted on shutdown
Mapping functions live in `src/adapter/mapping.rs` — one place for all `_core` → `_adp` conversions
Dates, prices, counts — format to display strings in the mapping, not in the UI component
banned: `_core` types in `AdapterState_sta` or passed to `ui.set_*()`
Computed display values (formatted strings, derived booleans) in `_core` types — compute in Adapter
Optional fields that hide loading or error state — make all states explicit

title: Core Design
subtitle: Core is pure business logic — same input, same output, no side effects, no platform
rules: Platform operations (file paths, clipboard, appearance) go through PAL — Core defines the need, PAL provides it
Core receives `AppConfig_cfg` and `CoreState_sta` as constructor parameters — never fetches them
One module per domain concern — one file, one responsibility, one `_core` type family
Core returns `Result<T, AppError_x>` — it never panics, never crashes silently
`_core` types are the domain vocabulary — `Field_core`, `Collection_core`, `Schema_core`
Validation of business rules belongs here — validation of input shape belongs in Adapter
banned: `use slint`, `use gtk`, `use winit`, `use tokio`, `use std::fs`, `use reqwest` in Core
Core knowing about screen names, view models, or UI structure
Core calling Gateway or Adapter — it only calls PAL when platform behaviour is needed
`_core` file importing a `_adp` type — Core must not know Adapter exists
`_core` file importing a `_ui` type — Core must not know UI exists
`_core` file importing a `_gtw` type — Core must not know IO exists

title: Core State
subtitle: CoreState_sta owns domain session data — caches, active sessions, computed aggregates
rules: `CoreState_sta` holds domain session state — computed results, active selections, caches
Gateway deserializes `CoreState_sta` from disk on startup and passes it to Core as a parameter
Core updates `CoreState_sta` internally — no other layer mutates it directly
Gateway serializes `CoreState_sta` to disk on shutdown via `Persistable_x`
`CoreState_sta` contains only domain types (`_core`, `_x`) — never view models (`_adp`)
`state_key()` matches the filename Gateway writes — `"core"` → `state/core.toml`
`Default` implementation is the first-run state — no separate init logic needed
banned: UI state (scroll, selection, active panel) in `CoreState_sta` — that belongs in `AdapterState_sta`
Config values in `CoreState_sta` — config is `_cfg`, state is `_sta`

title: PAL Design
subtitle: One trait per concern, one implementation per platform — platform code lives nowhere else
rules: One trait interface per concern — `FilePal_pal`, `WindowPal_pal`, `AppearancePal_pal`
Core and Gateway receive PAL as `Arc<dyn SomePal_pal>` — never import concrete implementations
Each target platform has one PAL implementation struct tagged `_pal`
PAL implementations are the ONLY place that imports OS-specific crates (`winapi`, `objc2`, `libc`)
Adding a new platform = adding one new `_pal` implementation struct — zero changes to Core or Gateway
iOS is a PAL implementation (`IosFilePal_pal`, `IosWindowPal_pal`) — not a separate architecture layer
Android is a PAL implementation — same pattern
Platform selection (`#[cfg]`) happens in `main.rs` — constructs the right PAL, injects via trait
After construction, the rest of the app sees only `Arc<dyn FilePal_pal>` — no platform conditionals
banned: `#[cfg(target_os)]` outside `src/pal/`
PAL implementations holding mutable state — stateless delegate only
Business logic or data transformation inside PAL — it executes, not decides
`_pal` file importing a `_core` type — PAL must not know domain exists
`_pal` file importing a `_adp` type — PAL must not know Adapter exists
`_pal` file importing a `_ui` type — PAL must not know UI exists
`_pal` file importing a `_gtw` type — PAL must not know Gateway exists

title: PAL Traits
subtitle: The contracts between Core/Gateway and the platform — implement once per target
rules: Every PAL trait method returns `Result<T, AppError_x>` — platform calls can always fail
Trait methods are narrow — one operation, no overloaded concerns
PAL traits live in `src/pal/traits.rs` — implementations in `src/pal/<platform>.rs`
Core and Gateway only call methods on these traits — never call OS APIs directly
`watch_appearance` runs in its own thread — sends changes via channel, Adapter posts to UI via `invoke_from_event_loop` (see slint/threading.md)
Unit tests for Core and Gateway use `TestFilePal_pal` — no real disk, no platform dependency
Test doubles live in `src/pal/` alongside real implementations

title: UI/UX Rules
subtitle: Perfect UI per platform — structure, state flow, and platform behaviour
sections: Paradigm — how to structure UI code
rules: One file per concern — component, token group, state type, feature area each get their own file

title: New App Checklist
subtitle: Verify before shipping — all platform UX requirements

title: UI Component Rules
subtitle: One file, one component, one job — small by default
rules: One component per file — file name matches component name
Compose via small components, never via deep nesting
Components are stateless by default — receive state in, emit events out
No business logic inside components — delegate to Adapter
HomeScreen shows the home — not home AND sidebar AND header
Extract a sub-component whenever a section can be named independently
Formatting, conditional rendering, computed labels → computed property in Adapter — not in the component
If you must scroll to understand a component → split it now
One screen component ≤ 3 levels of nesting visible at a glance
File name = component name, PascalCase (UserCard.tsx, UserCard.kt, user_card.py)
Screen/page entry points: `NameScreen` or `NamePage` — e.g. `HomeScreen`, `SettingsPage`
Reusable components: descriptive noun — `UserCard`, `ActionButton`, `LoadingSpinner`
Private sub-components may live in the same file only if they are ≤ 20 lines and used nowhere else
banned: Components that render multiple independent UI concerns
Inline conditional logic deeper than one level — extract to sub-component
Side effects inside render / composable / template body
God components that own multiple screens' worth of markup

title: Context Menus
subtitle: Right-click menus, primary menu, and minimum required items
rules: Right-click (secondary click) MUST show context menu if relevant actions exist
Context menu MUST also be accessible via `Shift+F10` or `Menu` key
Long press on touch = right-click equivalent
Menus should contain 3–12 items
Label commands with verbs: "Open", "Copy", "Delete"
Group related items with separators
Items must have access keys (underlined letter)
Every app MUST include in the primary menu:
banned: Nested submenus — flat structure only
Close/Quit in the primary menu

title: Drag and Drop
subtitle: File-to-terminal path insertion, file tree, and external sources
rules: Dragging a file from file tree into terminal inserts the file path at cursor
Drop targets MUST show visual feedback when drag hovers
GTK4: Use `:drop(active)` CSS pseudoclass
Invalid drop targets show "not allowed" cursor
Show drag icon representing what is being dragged
Every drag-and-drop action MUST have a keyboard/menu alternative

title: UI File Structure
subtitle: Folders by feature — each folder is a UI area, each file is one component
rules: One folder per UI area or screen
One component per file — never dump multiple components in one file
Folder name matches the area or screen it represents
Sub-components used only in one area live in that area's folder
Shared components used in 2+ areas live in `src/ui/shared/`
Screen file is the entry point — it composes from sub-components in its folder
When a component is needed in a second area → move it to `shared/`
Depth max 2: `src/ui/<area>/<file>` — no deeper nesting of folders
Toolkit template files (`.ui`, `.blueprint`) co-locate with their code file — same folder
File name = component name — no suffix like `UserCardComponent` or `UserCardView`
banned: Flat `ui/` folder with all components at the same level
Type-based folders (`src/ui/buttons/`, `src/ui/cards/`) — use feature folders
Multiple independent components in one file
Copying a component into a second folder — promote to shared instead

title: GTK4 — Rust + gtk-rs
subtitle: glib::clone!, composite templates, weak refs, signal handlers
rules: `glib::clone!` for closures capturing Rc/Arc — prevents strong-reference cycles in signal handlers
Composite templates for complex widgets — keep widget tree in .ui XML, not in Rust code
Weak references in signal handlers — never hold strong refs from signals
Actions for keyboard shortcuts — use `gio::SimpleAction`, not manual key handlers
State lives in a central `AppState_sta` struct — widgets only render, never own state
Widget callbacks emit events up — they do not mutate state directly
State mutations happen in the Adapter, results flow back to widgets via property bindings
One `.ui` file per widget — keeps the Rust file focused on behaviour only
Template children via `#[template_child]` — no manual widget lookup by ID

title: Help, About, and Shortcuts
subtitle: Every GUI app must have About, a license, and a shortcuts overview — delivered natively per platform
rules: About is reached from the primary menu (hamburger) → "About [AppName]"
Shortcuts overview is reached via `Ctrl+?` and from the primary menu
Help is reached via `F1` — minimum: opens shortcuts window or a help view
About must contain: app name, version, short description, author name, website, license
Group shortcuts by function area, not by key
List only app-specific shortcuts — skip universal ones unless they are non-obvious
Update shortcuts window whenever a shortcut is added or removed
banned: Shipping without About, license, or shortcuts overview
Custom About dialog on platforms that provide a standard one (GNOME, macOS, KDE)

title: Issue Reporter Integration
subtitle: Separate process for crash survival — shared across all apps
rules: Issue Reporter runs as its own process, NOT embedded in the host app
Launch as detached process — must survive host crash

title: Keyboard Shortcuts
subtitle: Standard shortcuts and navigation — GNOME HIG compliant
rules: Terminal emulators (VTE) intercept Ctrl+C/V — use Shift modifier
Use `Ctrl+Letter` as primary pattern
Use `Ctrl+Shift+Letter` for reverse/extended functions
Make shortcuts mnemonic (Ctrl+F = Find, Ctrl+S = Save)
App-specific shortcuts use `Ctrl+Shift+` prefix
Only assign shortcuts to commonly-used actions
Always use the standard shortcut if the function matches
Menu items show shortcut labels automatically via GTK/Qt actions
Tooltips on buttons mention shortcuts: `"Zen Mode (Ctrl+Shift+Z)"`
Context menus expose actions for right-click users
Shortcuts dialog (`Ctrl+?`) lists all available shortcuts
Header bar menu includes "Keyboard Shortcuts" entry
banned: `Super+*` — all Super combinations (GNOME Shell)
`Alt+Tab` — window switching
`Alt+F4` — close window
`Alt+F6/F7/F8` — window management
`Ctrl+Alt+Delete` — power off
`PrintScreen` variants — screenshots
Three/four-finger gestures — system gestures
`Alt+Letter` for shortcuts — conflicts with access keys
`Super` — reserved for GNOME Shell

title: Android Navigation — Kotlin + Jetpack Compose
subtitle: No menubar on Android — Bottom Navigation + Top App Bar overflow menu
rules: Top App Bar overflow menu (three-dot) contains: Settings, Help, About, Report Issue
Settings opens a dedicated Settings screen via NavController — not a dialog
About is a full-screen composable or AlertDialog
Back navigation is handled by the system — do NOT add explicit back buttons to top-level screens
Use NavController + NavHost for all navigation — no manual Fragment transactions
Switch to NavigationDrawer when destination count exceeds 5 — do not squeeze BottomNav
About screen shows: app icon, name, version, description, website link, license link
Website and license are tappable — not plain text
banned: Traditional menubar widget on Android
BottomNavigationBar with more than 5 items
Mixing Fragment transactions with NavController
Opening Settings as a Dialog — it must be a full screen

title: GNOME Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: No native menubar on GNOME — use HeaderBar with hamburger PopupMenu in Slint
rules: Hamburger menu (≡) in top-right of HeaderBar contains: Shortcuts, Preferences, Help, About
About is a Slint dialog (no GTK AdwAboutDialog available from pure Rust/Slint)
Shortcuts window is a Slint PopupWindow or Dialog — opened by Ctrl+?
Register Ctrl+? and F1 as Slint key handlers — no muda needed on Linux
banned: Native GTK menubar — GNOME HIG explicitly forbids traditional menubars
Putting Quit in the hamburger menu — Ctrl+Q is sufficient, Quit belongs nowhere in GNOME UI

title: iOS Navigation — SwiftUI
subtitle: No menubar on iOS — TabView for primary nav, toolbar items for secondary actions
rules: Secondary actions go in .toolbar { ToolbarItem } — not in a custom overlay
About is presented as a sheet or pushed NavigationLink
Settings belong in a dedicated Settings screen pushed via NavigationLink
Use .contextMenu for long-press actions on list items
Back navigation is automatic via NavigationStack — no custom back button on standard screens
Use `@AppStorage` for simple persistent settings — no manual UserDefaults
Present settings as a sheet (modal) from toolbar — not a tab item
About shows: app icon, name, version, description, website link, license link
Use `Link` for website and license — tappable, opens in Safari
Destructive context menu actions must use `role: .destructive`
banned: Custom menubar widget on iOS
TabView with more than 5 tabs without restructuring
Putting app settings inside an alert or sheet — use a full NavigationLink screen

title: KDE Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: Hamburger menu by default — optional global menubar export via DBus for Plasma
sections: Optional: Global Menu via muda (Plasma only)
rules: Default to hamburger menu in HeaderBar — same pattern as GNOME
If exporting to global menu: use `muda` with the `dbus` feature on Linux
Shortcuts use `Ctrl+?`, Preferences use `Ctrl+,`, Quit uses `Ctrl+Q`
About is a Slint dialog — same component as GNOME (reuse AboutDialog.slint)
Shortcuts window is a Slint dialog — same component as GNOME (reuse ShortcutsWindow.slint)
Always fall back to hamburger if global menu DBus service is unavailable
Never require global menu — treat it as progressive enhancement
banned: Assuming global menu is always present — many KDE users disable it

title: macOS Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: NSMenu via muda — the menubar lives at the top of the screen, always
rules: Preferences uses `Cmd+,` — not `Ctrl+,`
Quit uses `Cmd+Q` — PredefinedMenuItem::quit() sets this automatically
Shortcuts window uses `Cmd+?` (Shift+Cmd+/)
Menu is installed before `ui.run()` — after the window handle is available
Always set name, version, authors, website, license, copyright in AboutMetadata
issue_url optional but recommended — shows as "Report a Bug" link on macOS
banned: Ctrl+Q or Ctrl+, on macOS — use Cmd modifiers
Custom drawn menubar — NSMenu is mandatory on macOS

title: Qt6 Menus — C++ / QML
subtitle: QMenuBar on desktop — platform-native delivery on macOS and Windows automatically
rules: File > Preferences on Windows/Linux — Application menu on macOS (Qt handles this via QAction::ApplicationSpecificRole)
Use QKeySequence::StandardKey for all shortcuts — never hardcode platform-specific keys
QML: use MenuBar { Menu { Action {} } } — not custom-drawn overlays
About dialog must show: name, version, description, website, license
Always use `StandardKey.*` enum values — Qt maps them per platform automatically
`setMenuRole` / Action role routes items to the macOS Application menu without #ifdef
About shows: name, version, description, website link, license link
Website and license are clickable links via `onLinkActivated`
banned: Custom drawn menubar widget replacing QMenuBar / MenuBar
Hardcoded key strings (e.g. "Ctrl+,") when QKeySequence::Preferences exists
Separate About actions for macOS and non-macOS — set the role and Qt routes it

title: Menus in Rust + Slint
subtitle: Native menus via `muda` on macOS/Windows — Slint-native on Linux
rules: `muda` is the crate for native menus in Rust — works with Slint's winit backend
Menu structure (items, labels, shortcuts) is identical across platforms — only delivery differs
All menu events are handled in the main Rust event loop, not in Slint callbacks
About, Shortcuts, and Help are required in every app — see help-about.md
banned: Custom drawn menubar on macOS or Windows — use native
Blocking the Slint event loop with menu callbacks

title: Windows Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: Win32 native menu via muda — attached to the window frame
rules: Use `Ctrl+,` for Preferences (Windows convention, not Cmd)
Use `Ctrl+Q` for Quit (Windows convention)
Use `Ctrl+?` for Shortcuts (Ctrl+Shift+/)
Menu is attached to the HWND after Slint creates the window
About dialog is a custom ContentDialog-equivalent — no native standard exists on Windows
About dialog shows: name, version, description, author, website, license — all fields
Website and license are clickable links, not plain text
banned: Alt+F4 in the menu — it is a system shortcut, already works without listing it

title: Pointer and Touch
subtitle: Click behavior, hover rules, touch interaction
rules: Single click to activate — GNOME does not use double-click for primary actions
Click targets must be large enough for comfortable use
Hover MUST NOT reveal essential actions or information (inaccessible on touch)
Tooltips are acceptable (supplementary info only)
Long press = right-click (context menu)
Two-finger gestures available for app use (pinch zoom, two-finger scroll)
banned: Simultaneous multi-button presses
Referencing specific devices in UI text (no "click", use "select"/"activate")
Hover-only menus or buttons
Three/four-finger gestures — reserved for system

title: UI State Flow
subtitle: State in from Adapter, events out to Adapter — components never own domain state
rules: Components are pure renderers — given the same state, they render the same output
The Adapter owns AdapterState_sta — UI reads it, never mutates it
Every loading, error, and empty state must be handled — no silent blanks
Local UI state (focus, hover, open/closed) is the only state a component may own
One-way data flow — state flows down, events flow up
Components declare what they need (typed props/state-in) — never reach up for it
Events carry only the data needed — not the full state object
`ItemViewModel_adp` is a flat, UI-ready struct — no domain objects reach the component
Status flags (`is_loading`, `error`, `is_empty`) are always explicit — never inferred in the component
Every component that shows async data MUST handle: loading, error, empty, and content

subtitle: module.yaml — no Gradle for desktop, minimal config
rules: `module.yaml` for configuration (no Gradle)
Minimal dependencies
Use JetBrains Compose dependencies (`$compose.*`)

title: Coroutines
subtitle: Dispatchers, never block main thread
rules: `Dispatchers.IO` for I/O operations
`Dispatchers.Default` for CPU-intensive work
`Dispatchers.Main` only for emitting results to the UI layer — logic stays on IO/Default
Never block main thread — no `runBlocking` in production code
Use `SupervisorJob()` in long-lived scopes so one child failure does not cancel siblings

title: Immutable Data Classes
subtitle: val only, default values, copy for modifications
rules: Immutable data classes — `val` only
Default values for optional fields
`copy()` for modifications
No logic in data classes
banned: Mutable public properties
`var` for state (use MutableStateFlow)

title: Encapsulation
subtitle: Private by default — platform layer hides Java imports
rules: Private by default — only expose what's needed
Internal for module-level visibility
Use interfaces for abstraction
No public mutable state
ALL `java.*` and `javax.*` imports MUST be in `platform/` layer
`domain/` and `ui/` MUST NOT import `java.*` or `javax.*` directly
Platform layer exposes ONLY Kotlin types
Platform components initialize ONCE at app start
banned: `java.io.*` imports in domain/ or ui/
`javax.swing.*` imports outside platform/
`java.awt.*` imports outside platform/
Passing `java.io.File` across layer boundaries (use String paths)
Initializing Swing LookAndFeel per-call (causes state corruption)
`!!` non-null assertion — use `?: return`, `?: throw`, or restructure to non-nullable
Multiple top-level classes in one file — one class per file

title: Ktor Client
subtitle: Single HttpClient instance, ContentNegotiation, error handling
rules: Single HttpClient instance per app
ContentNegotiation for JSON
Proper error handling

title: Kotlin Quick Reference
subtitle: All rules at a glance

title: Result Pattern
subtitle: Sealed class Result — no exceptions for expected failures
rules: No exceptions for expected failures
Sealed class Result for operations that can fail
Handle all cases explicitly

title: Build Stability Guardrails
subtitle: Version pinning, CI truth, cache hygiene
sections: CI as Single Source of Truth
rules: Pin ALL versions explicitly — Kotlin, Compose, AGP, JDK
Never use floating versions (`1.+`, `latest.*`)
Upgrade one dependency at a time, verify CI green
CI preflight build on every PR
CI builds clean (no local caches)
Build all targets: Android + JVM/Desktop
If CI green = it works, local issues are cache problems
Don't debug "ghost errors" — clear cache first
If error persists after cache clear, it's a real bug
Read actual error, not just "build failed"
Test the Adapter layer independently — inject a fake repository
Use fakes, not mocks — fake implements the interface, mock guesses call order
Test state transitions — verify that input produces the correct output state
banned: Mixing JDK versions between IDE and CLI

title: C# / .NET Rules
subtitle: .NET 9, C# 13, nullable-on — cross-platform first
rules: Modern C# 13 idioms — nullable reference types always enabled
Cross-platform first — no Windows-only APIs unless platform-gated
Records for data, classes for behavior — immutability by default
Async/await everywhere — no blocking calls on UI or async paths
Result-typed errors in domain code — exceptions only at system boundaries
One class per file — nesting = namespace of files, not nested classes
Stateless logic — all app state in a central `AppState_sta` type; functions transform, never store state
Internal by default — `public` only at assembly boundary

title: Error Handling
subtitle: Result types in domain code — exceptions only at system boundaries
rules: Domain code returns `Result<T>` (or `OneOf<T, TError>`) — not exceptions
Exceptions for truly unexpected system failures only (I/O, DB unavailable)
Never catch-and-swallow — every catch has a named recovery action
See `global/error-flow.md` for taxonomy (Transient/UserError/SystemError/Bug)
Validation errors are domain results, not exceptions
banned: `catch (Exception ex) { }` — swallowing without recovery
Exceptions for control flow (validation failures, not-found)
`throw new Exception(...)` — use typed exception subclasses
`NullReferenceException` as intentional design — use nullable types properly
Wildcard catch that logs but does not recover or rethrow

title: C# / .NET Project Initialization
subtitle: Run this INSTEAD OF improvising — every C# project starts here
banned: Writing any domain code before `dotnet build` is green
Skipping `Directory.Build.props` — nullable and analyzers are non-negotiable
One `.csproj` for everything — Core must be separate from Infrastructure
Putting business logic in the entry point project (Cli/Api/UI)
Skipping proj/ files — init is not done until all proj/ files exist

title: LINQ
subtitle: Method syntax, deferred execution — explicit materialisation
rules: Method syntax preferred over query syntax (consistent with codebase style)
LINQ is lazy — materialise (`ToList()`, `ToArray()`) only when needed
Never use LINQ inside tight loops on hot paths — profile first
`IAsyncEnumerable<T>` + `await foreach` for async streaming collections
Break long LINQ chains into named intermediate steps
banned: `First()` without null check — use `FirstOrDefault()` and handle null
`Count() > 0` — use `Any()`
`Select(...).Where(...)` when `Where(...).Select(...)` is clearer
LINQ to materialise only to iterate once — keep as `IEnumerable<T>`
Side effects inside LINQ expressions (`Select(x => { Log(x); return x; })`)

title: Module Structure
subtitle: One class per file — internal by default, public only at assembly boundary
rules: One top-level type per file (class, record, interface, enum)
File name matches type name exactly
`internal` for intra-assembly types — `public` only at assembly boundary
No circular dependencies between projects
Max ~300 LOC per file
Max ~10 public types per assembly
No `Utilities`, `Helpers`, `Common` namespaces without explicit domain
Namespace matches folder path: `MyApp.Core.Users`
Use file-scoped namespaces (`namespace MyApp.Core.Users;`) — not block-scoped

title: Naming Rules
subtitle: Anti-variable-noise + layer-tag convention
rules: A name must explain WHY the variable exists — not just WHAT it contains
Names must reflect phase:
Scope < 5 lines: short names ok (`i`, `id`, `len`)
Scope >= 5 lines: semantic names REQUIRED
Plural MANDATORY for collections: `users`, `tokens`
Iterator uses role, not type: `foreach (var user in users)`
Must start with `is`, `has`, `can`, `should`
Verbs first
No type-leak in name
All types use a layer-tag suffix matching their architectural role
Tag = folder the type lives in — see [global/topology.md](../global/topology.md) for the canonical tag list
Interfaces use `I` prefix: `IUserRepository_adp`, `IEventBus_x`
The layer-tag comes AFTER the I-prefixed name
banned: `data`, `info`, `value`, `item`, `object`, `temp`, `state`, `ctx`, `result`, `res`, `var`, `obj`
`GetUserData()` → GOOD: `LoadUser()`
Concrete class named `ISomething` — that prefix is reserved for interfaces

title: Flat Code — C#
subtitle: See [global/nesting.md](../global/nesting.md) for shared rules
rules: Guard clauses at top — return/throw early for preconditions
Max 3 levels of nesting — extract method if deeper
Prefer `switch` expressions over chains of `if/else if`
banned: `else` after a `return` or `throw`
More than 3 levels of `{ }` nesting
`_ => { }` switch arm that silently discards — always throw or log+recover

title: C# Quick Reference
subtitle: All rules at a glance

title: Threading
subtitle: async/await everywhere — CancellationToken mandatory, no blocking on async paths
rules: All I/O operations are async — no `.Result`, `.Wait()`, or `.GetAwaiter().GetResult()` on async paths
`CancellationToken` parameter on every async public method
`System.Threading.Channels` for producer/consumer pipelines
`SemaphoreSlim` for async-safe mutual exclusion
`IProgress<T>` for progress reporting — not events from background threads
All spawned tasks must be stoppable via CancellationToken
UI updates only on dispatcher thread — use `DispatcherQueue.TryEnqueue()`
Never block the UI thread — `await` all async work
banned: `.Result` or `.Wait()` on a `Task` inside async context — causes deadlocks
`Thread.Sleep` — use `await Task.Delay(ms, ct)`
`async void` — use `async Task` except for event handlers (document why)
Fire-and-forget `Task.Run(...)` without storing and cancelling the task
Shared mutable state without synchronization primitive

title: Type Safety
subtitle: Records for data, nullable always on, strong typing everywhere
rules: Enable nullable reference types globally — `<Nullable>enable</Nullable>` in every `.csproj`
`record` for immutable data transfer objects and value objects
`struct` / `readonly struct` for small value types with no identity
Newtype pattern via `record struct` for distinct IDs
`T?` for nullable values — never sentinel values (`-1`, `""`, `null` object patterns)
Generics with constraints instead of `object`
banned: Nullable reference types disabled (`#nullable disable` without explicit reason)
`dynamic` — use proper generics or interfaces
Sentinel values (`-1`, `""`, `new User()` as "not found")
`object` as function parameter or return type — use generics or interfaces
Mutable public properties on records used as value objects
`var` when the type is not obvious from the right-hand side

subtitle: Gating levels — local, merge, release
